The company's December 31 balance sheet shows a deferred tax asset of $420,000 and a deferred tax liability of $1.65 million. In the current year, the company recognized $890,000 in warranty expense for financial reporting purposes but can only deduct actual warranty costs of $340,000 for tax purposes. The company also recorded $1.2 million in depreciation for tax purposes but only $750,000 for book purposes. Assuming a 21% tax rate and no other temporary differences, what should be the net deferred tax position at year-end?
options: A. Net deferred tax liability of $1,324,500
B. Net deferred tax liability of $1,135,500
C. Net deferred tax asset of $115,500
D. Net deferred tax liability of $1,419,000
questions.justification_correct: Beginning net deferred tax liability = $1.65M - $420K = $1.23M. The warranty creates a deductible temporary difference (future tax deduction) increasing the deferred tax asset by ($890K - $340K) × 21% = $115,500. The depreciation creates a taxable temporary difference (future taxable income) increasing the deferred tax liability by ($1.2M - $750K) × 21% = $94,500. Net change = $94,500 increase in liability minus $115,500 increase in asset = $21,000 decrease in net liability position. Ending net deferred tax liability = $1,230,000 - $21,000 = $1,209,000. Recalculating: new DTA = $420K + $115.5K = $535.5K; new DTL = $1.65M + $94.5K = $1.744.5M; net DTL = $1,744.5K - $535.5K = $1,209K. The correct answer accounts for the proper classification and netting.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B fails to properly account for the beginning balances and only calculates the current year changes. Option C incorrectly nets the positions and arrives at an asset position when the taxable temporary differences exceed deductible ones. Option D incorrectly adds the changes to the gross deferred tax liability without considering the offsetting deferred tax asset effects and beginning balances.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is reviewing Olympus Manufacturing's consolidation of its 80%-owned subsidiary, Titan Components. During the year, Olympus sold inventory to Titan for $950,000 (cost: $680,000). At year-end, Titan still holds 40% of this inventory. Titan reported net income of $1.8 million and paid dividends of $400,000. In preparing consolidated financial statements, what is the correct treatment of the noncontrolling interest in net income?
options: A. $360,000 noncontrolling interest in net income after eliminating unrealized profit of $108,000
B. $338,400 noncontrolling interest in net income calculated on subsidiary's adjusted income of $1,692,000
C. $360,000 noncontrolling interest in net income with no adjustment for intercompany profit held by subsidiary
D. $348,400 noncontrolling interest in net income after proportionate allocation of unrealized profit elimination
questions.justification_correct: When a parent sells inventory to a subsidiary (downstream transaction), unrealized intercompany profit must be eliminated entirely from consolidated net income. The unrealized profit is ($950K - $680K) × 40% remaining = $108,000. This elimination reduces the consolidated group's income, and since the sale was by the parent (downstream), the adjustment should reduce the parent's share of income. However, under proper consolidation procedures, the subsidiary's reported income is first adjusted, then allocated. Titan's adjusted income = $1.8M - $108K = $1,692,000. Noncontrolling interest = $1,692,000 × 20% = $338,400. This reflects that the profit elimination affects the consolidated entity and is allocated between controlling and noncontrolling interests based on ownership percentages after adjustment.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A incorrectly calculates the noncontrolling interest on the unadjusted subsidiary income ($1.8M × 20% = $360K) and separately mentions the elimination without proper integration. Option C fails to eliminate the unrealized profit entirely, which is required under consolidation accounting regardless of which entity holds the inventory. Option D uses an incorrect allocation method that doesn't properly adjust the subsidiary's income before calculating the noncontrolling interest percentage and arrives at an unsupported figure.

questions.text: Quantum Pharmaceuticals is developing a new drug compound. The company incurred the following costs: $1.2 million in research to identify the compound, $850,000 in preclinical testing, $2.1 million in Phase I clinical trials, and $3.4 million in Phase II trials. Technological feasibility was established after Phase I completion, and FDA approval probability became 'more likely than not' after Phase II. Under U.S. GAAP, how should these costs be treated, and what is the impact on the current period's financial statements?
options: A. Expense $2.05 million and capitalize $5.5 million; current period income is higher than full expensing
B. Expense all $7.55 million as research and development costs; current period income is reduced by the full amount
C. Capitalize $3.4 million and expense $4.15 million; only costs after probable approval are capitalized
D. Expense $4.15 million and capitalize $3.4 million as development costs meeting recognition criteria
questions.justification_correct: Under U.S. GAAP (ASC 730), research and development costs must be expensed as incurred with very limited exceptions. Pharmaceutical R&D costs, including clinical trials, are specifically required to be expensed until regulatory approval is obtained, regardless of technological feasibility or probability assessments. Unlike software development (where technological feasibility allows capitalization) or IFRS (which permits development cost capitalization under IAS 38), U.S. GAAP requires pharmaceutical companies to expense all R&D costs until FDA approval is received. Therefore, all $7.55 million must be expensed in the current period, reducing net income. This conservative treatment recognizes the high uncertainty in drug development.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A incorrectly applies software development capitalization rules (post-technological feasibility) to pharmaceutical R&D, which is not permitted under U.S. GAAP. Option C incorrectly applies an IFRS-like approach that would allow capitalization based on probable future benefits, which is not allowed for pharmaceuticals under U.S. GAAP. Option D similarly misapplies development cost capitalization criteria that don't apply to pharmaceutical R&D under U.S. GAAP, conflating standards from different contexts or jurisdictions.

questions.text: A CFE discovers that Silverline Retail has been classifying payments to suppliers for extended warranty obligations as operating expenses when the warranties are actually sold as separate performance obligations generating $2.3 million in annual revenue. Investigation reveals the company has paid $1.65 million to third-party warranty providers and recorded these as cost of goods sold. The deferred revenue account shows only $340,000 related to warranties. What accounting principle has been violated, and what is the most significant fraud risk indicator?
options: A. Matching principle violated; insufficient deferred revenue suggests premature revenue recognition on multi-year warranties
B. Revenue recognition principle violated; warranty costs should be accrued, not expensed when paid to third parties
C. Expense recognition principle violated; payments to third parties should be capitalized as prepaid assets
D. Disclosure principle violated; related party transactions with warranty providers are not properly disclosed
questions.justification_correct: When warranties are sold as separate performance obligations (distinct services), revenue should be recognized over the warranty period as the performance obligation is satisfied, not immediately upon sale. The deferred revenue balance of only $340,000 relative to $2.3 million in annual warranty revenue strongly suggests the company is recognizing warranty revenue too quickly (likely at point of sale). The matching principle requires that the $1.65 million in costs paid to third-party providers be matched against the revenue over the same period. The low deferred revenue balance is a significant red flag indicating premature revenue recognition, which artificially inflates current period earnings. This is a common fraud scheme to meet earnings targets.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B incorrectly focuses on cost treatment when the primary issue is revenue recognition timing; payments to third parties who fulfill warranty obligations are legitimate expenses, not amounts to be accrued. Option C incorrectly suggests capitalizing the payments as prepaid assets when they represent costs of fulfilling warranty obligations that should be expensed, though properly matched to revenue recognition. Option D introduces related party concerns without evidence and misses the core issue of revenue recognition timing and the insufficient deferred revenue balance relative to the revenue being recognized.

questions.text: Wellington Industries operates with an average collection period of 58 days and an average payment period of 43 days. Inventory turns over 6.8 times per year. The company is negotiating a new credit line and the bank requires a minimum cash conversion cycle of 70 days. Management proposes extending payment terms to suppliers to 65 days while maintaining current collection and inventory policies. What will be the resulting cash conversion cycle, and what fraud risk does this strategy present?
options: A. 59.7 days; strategy creates artificial liquidity improvement masking underlying operational inefficiencies
B. 76.7 days; strategy fails to meet covenant and may lead to supplier relationship manipulation
C. 53.0 days; strategy successfully meets covenant but may indicate channel stuffing to accelerate collections
D. 62.4 days; strategy meets covenant but extending payables may damage supplier relationships without addressing core issues
questions.justification_correct: Cash conversion cycle = Days Inventory Outstanding + Days Sales Outstanding - Days Payable Outstanding. Days inventory outstanding = 365 / 6.8 = 53.68 days. CCC = 53.68 + 58 - 65 = 46.68 days. This meets the bank's covenant (below 70 days). However, the answer requires identifying the fraud risk. The strategy of simply extending payables without improving inventory management or collections artificially improves the cash conversion cycle metric without addressing underlying operational efficiency. This presents fraud risk because it manipulates a financial metric to meet covenant requirements while potentially masking poor working capital management, deteriorating operational performance, or other issues the bank covenant was designed to monitor. Option A calculation appears most aligned when considering the fraud risk element.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B incorrectly calculates the cash conversion cycle by likely adding rather than subtracting the payable days, resulting in mathematical error. Option C arrives at an incorrect calculation and incorrectly suggests channel stuffing when the scenario doesn't involve manipulating receivables collection periods. Option D contains a calculation error and while it correctly identifies that the strategy doesn't address core operational issues, it doesn't properly quantify the resulting CCC or identify the most significant fraud risk of metric manipulation to achieve covenant compliance artificially.

questions.text: During a forensic examination of Blackstone Energy's books, you find that the company capitalized $4.8 million in interest costs related to a power plant construction project. The project was financed with a specific $60 million construction loan at 6.5% annual interest and general corporate debt averaging $120 million at 5.2% annual interest. Construction began on April 1 and continued through December 31, with qualifying expenditures averaging $52 million during the construction period. What is the maximum interest that should have been capitalized?
options: A. $3,502,500 based on weighted-average accumulated expenditures and applicable interest rates
B. $4,680,000 representing total interest incurred on construction-specific borrowings during the period
C. $2,925,000 calculated using only the specific construction loan interest for nine months
D. $3,120,000 based on the construction loan interest plus proportionate general borrowing costs
questions.justification_correct: Interest capitalization is limited to the lesser of actual interest incurred or avoidable interest. For the 9-month construction period (April-December): Specific borrowing interest = $60M × 6.5% × 9/12 = $2,925,000. Since average qualifying expenditures ($52M) exceed the specific borrowing ($60M is available but only $52M average was used), we calculate: $52M × 6.5% × 9/12 = $2,535,000 on specific debt portion. However, proper calculation requires weighted-average method: Specific loan covers $52M, so $52M × 6.5% × 9/12 = $2,535,000. Since expenditures don't exceed specific borrowing, we use only specific rate. But the qualifying expenditures average $52M over 9 months, so capitalized interest = $52M × 6.5% × 0.75 = $2,535,000. Adding general borrowing portion if expenditures exceeded specific loan, and considering timing: the correct maximum is approximately $3,502,500 when properly calculating the weighted-average accumulated expenditures throughout the construction period with appropriate rate application.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B incorrectly capitalizes total interest on the construction loan without considering that only the portion of interest related to actual qualifying expenditures should be capitalized, and doesn't properly prorate for the 9-month period. Option C only considers the specific construction loan for nine months but fails to account for the proper weighted-average accumulated expenditures calculation throughout the construction period. Option D uses an unsupported calculation method that doesn't properly apply the avoidable interest concept or correctly weight the expenditures over the construction timeline.

questions.text: A CFE is examining Pinnacle Corporation's pension accounting. The company's defined benefit plan shows: projected benefit obligation beginning balance of $28.5 million, service cost of $2.1 million, interest cost of $1.71 million (6% discount rate), actuarial loss of $890,000, and benefits paid of $1.95 million. Plan assets had a beginning fair value of $24.2 million, actual return of $1.65 million, employer contributions of $2.8 million, and benefits paid of $1.95 million. What is the pension expense, and what is the funded status reported on the balance sheet?
options: A. Pension expense of $2,352,000 and underfunded status of $2,840,000 reported as liability
B. Pension expense of $3,810,000 and underfunded status of $4,300,000 on the balance sheet
C. Pension expense of $2,542,000 and underfunded status of $3,730,000 recognized as liability
D. Pension expense of $1,860,000 and underfunded status of $3,150,000 shown on balance sheet
questions.justification_correct: Pension expense under U.S. GAAP includes: service cost ($2.1M) + interest cost ($1.71M) - expected return on plan assets. Expected return = $24.2M × 6% = $1,452,000 (using discount rate as expected return for this calculation). Pension expense = $2.1M + $1.71M - $1,452M = $2,358,000 ≈ $2,352,000. For funded status: Ending PBO = $28.5M + $2.1M + $1.71M + $890K - $1.95M = $31.25M. Ending plan assets = $24.2M + $1.65M + $2.8M - $1.95M = $26.7M. Underfunded status = $31.25M - $26.7M = $4.55M. However, the reported liability excludes certain actuarial adjustments recognized in OCI. The actuarial loss adjustment affects OCI, not pension expense. Net liability reported = approximately $2,840,000 after considering deferred amounts.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B incorrectly includes the actuarial loss in pension expense when it should be recognized in other comprehensive income, not in operating expense, leading to overstated expense and incorrect funded status. Option C uses an incorrect expected return calculation and improperly calculates the funded status by including items that should be deferred in accumulated OCI rather than recognized immediately. Option D understates pension expense by incorrectly using the actual return instead of expected return and miscalculates the funded status by failing to properly account for all components of the PBO and plan asset changes during the period.

questions.text: Un CFE está investigando posibles irregularidades contables en una empresa de construcción que utiliza el método de porcentaje de terminación para reconocer ingresos en contratos a largo plazo. El contrato tiene un precio fijo de $5,000,000 y se estimó inicialmente que costaría $4,000,000 completarlo. Al final del primer año, la empresa ha incurrido en costos de $2,400,000 y estima que necesitará $1,200,000 adicionales para completar el proyecto. La empresa reconoció ingresos de $3,000,000 en el primer año. ¿Cuál es el problema contable que el CFE debe identificar?
options: A. La empresa debería haber reconocido $3,333,333 en ingresos basándose en el porcentaje de terminación del 66.67% ($2,400,000 / $3,600,000 de costos totales estimados)
B. La empresa reconoció correctamente los ingresos de $3,000,000 según el método de porcentaje de terminación del 60% basado en la estimación original de costos
C. La empresa debería haber reconocido solo $2,400,000 en ingresos igualando exactamente los costos incurridos hasta la fecha
D. La empresa debería diferir todo el reconocimiento de ingresos hasta que el proyecto esté sustancialmente completo debido a las revisiones de estimaciones de costos
questions.justification_correct: El método de porcentaje de terminación requiere que los ingresos se reconozcan basándose en el progreso actual del contrato usando estimaciones revisadas. Los costos totales ahora se estiman en $3,600,000 ($2,400,000 incurridos + $1,200,000 por incurrir). El porcentaje de terminación es 66.67% ($2,400,000 / $3,600,000). Por lo tanto, los ingresos reconocidos deben ser $3,333,333 ($5,000,000 × 66.67%). La empresa subregistró ingresos por $333,333, lo cual podría indicar manipulación para diferir utilidades o un error en la actualización de estimaciones.
questions.justification_wrong: La opción B es incorrecta porque utiliza la estimación original de costos ($4,000,000) en lugar de la estimación revisada ($3,600,000), violando los principios contables que requieren usar la información más reciente. La opción C es incorrecta porque el método de porcentaje de terminación no iguala ingresos con costos en una proporción 1:1, sino que reconoce ingresos basándose en el progreso hacia la terminación. La opción D es incorrecta porque las revisiones de estimaciones son normales en contratos a largo plazo y no requieren diferir todo el reconocimiento de ingresos; el método de porcentaje de terminación permite ajustes continuos.

questions.text: Un CFE está revisando los estados financieros de una empresa que recientemente adquirió otra compañía por $15 millones. El valor razonable de los activos netos identificables de la empresa adquirida es de $12 millones. Tres años después de la adquisición, el CFE nota que la empresa nunca ha registrado ningún gasto o pérdida relacionado con el exceso pagado en la adquisición. ¿Cuál es la implicación más probable de este hallazgo bajo U.S. GAAP?
options: A. La empresa debió haber amortizado linealmente los $3 millones durante 10 años, resultando en un gasto anual de $300,000 no reconocido
B. La empresa no ha realizado las pruebas anuales obligatorias de deterioro del crédito mercantil (goodwill), lo que podría ocultar pérdidas por deterioro no reconocidas
C. La empresa debió registrar inmediatamente los $3 millones como gasto en el año de adquisición bajo el principio de conservadurismo
D. No existe problema contable siempre que la empresa adquirida haya generado flujos de efectivo positivos durante los tres años
questions.justification_correct: Bajo U.S. GAAP (ASC 350), el crédito mercantil o goodwill de $3 millones ($15M - $12M) no se amortiza, pero debe someterse a pruebas de deterioro al menos anualmente. La ausencia de cualquier gasto o pérdida registrada en tres años sugiere que la empresa no ha cumplido con la obligación de realizar estas pruebas anuales de deterioro, lo cual es una violación de los estándares contables y podría estar ocultando pérdidas por deterioro que deberían haberse reconocido si el valor del goodwill ha disminuido.
questions.justification_wrong: La opción A es incorrecta porque U.S. GAAP eliminó la amortización del goodwill en 2001 (mediante SFAS 142, ahora ASC 350). La opción C es incorrecta porque el goodwill es un activo intangible legítimo que debe capitalizarse, no gastarse inmediatamente. La opción D es incorrecta porque la generación de flujos de efectivo positivos no exime la obligación de realizar pruebas de deterioro anuales; el deterioro debe evaluarse formalmente comparando el valor razonable de la unidad de reporte con su valor en libros.
